title: Laravel Livewire v3 Unauthenticated Remote Code Execution Actively Exploited by Iranian APT MuddyWater: CISA Adds CVE-2025-54068 to KEV Affecting Millions of PHP Web Applications with Federal Patch Deadline April 3, 2026
affected_products: All PHP web applications using affected Livewire v3 versions

sources.name: CISA: Adds Five Known Exploited Vulnerabilities to Catalog (March 20, 2026)
GitHub Security Advisory GHSA-29cq-5w36-x7w3 - Laravel Livewire
Laravel News: Livewire Security Advisory - CVE-2025-54068
Securing Laravel: CVE-2025-54068 - Critical Unauthenticated RCE in Livewire v3
Synacktiv Technical Analysis: CVE-2025-54068 Laravel Livewire Prop Hydration RCE
Wiz: CVE-2025-54068 Vulnerability Analysis - Laravel Livewire RCE
The Hacker News: CISA Flags Apple, Craft CMS, Laravel Bugs in KEV
SecurityOnline: CISA Adds CVE-2025-54068 Laravel Livewire to KEV

timeline.event: CVE-2025-54068 publicly disclosed; Laravel Livewire releases patched version 3.6.4. Synacktiv publishes technical analysis of the prop hydration bypass. INCIBE-CERT and other national CERTs issue advisories.
Livepyre public proof-of-concept exploit tool released by Synacktiv; multiple independent PoC exploits appear on GitHub and security research blogs within days
Widespread opportunistic exploitation scanning begins against PHP/Laravel installations worldwide; automated scanners target Livewire endpoints
MuddyWater (Iranian MOIS APT) begins targeted exploitation campaigns against government, energy, and financial sector organizations running vulnerable Laravel Livewire applications
US and European CERT organizations observe sustained MuddyWater exploitation campaigns leveraging CVE-2025-54068 for initial access to target networks
CISA adds CVE-2025-54068 to the Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog confirming active exploitation; federal agencies ordered to patch by April 3, 2026

simple_summary.what_happened: A critical vulnerability in Laravel Livewire v3, a popular PHP web framework component used by millions of websites worldwide, allows attackers to execute arbitrary code on web servers without any authentication. The flaw exists in how Livewire handles 'component hydration' — the process of restoring component state from client-side data. By manipulating this data, an attacker can inject malicious PHP objects that get executed server-side. Disclosed in July 2025, the vulnerability went largely unpatched across the internet and is now being actively exploited by MuddyWater, a state-sponsored Iranian hacking group aligned with Iran's Ministry of Intelligence and Security (MOIS). CISA added it to their Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog on March 20, 2026.
simple_summary.who_is_affected: Any website or web application built with Laravel Livewire v3 versions 3.0.0-beta.1 through 3.6.3 is vulnerable. Laravel is one of the most popular PHP web frameworks with millions of deployments globally. Affected sectors include government portals, banking and financial services, e-commerce platforms, healthcare portals, and enterprise internal web applications. Organizations that use Laravel for customer-facing or internal applications are at direct risk of complete server compromise.
simple_summary.what_attackers_do: Step 1: Attacker identifies a target web application using Laravel Livewire v3 (below version 3.6.4) — this can be done through Shodan scanning, Google dorking, or by simply browsing the site and noticing Livewire component signatures in HTML source code. Step 2: Attacker sends a crafted HTTP POST request to the Livewire component update endpoint, embedding a maliciously serialized PHP object within the 'snapshot' parameter that handles component state. Step 3: The Livewire server-side hydration process deserializes the attacker's payload without proper validation, triggering PHP object instantiation with attacker-controlled data. Step 4: The deserialized PHP object executes an attacker-specified PHP command or loads a remote webshell onto the server with the web server process privileges (often www-data or apache). Step 5: Attacker establishes persistent access, exfiltrates database credentials and application secrets, pivots to internal network resources, and optionally deploys ransomware or backdoors.
what_you_should_do: Immediately update Laravel Livewire to version 3.6.4 or later in all PHP applications
Audit all PHP/Laravel web applications in your environment to identify any using vulnerable Livewire v3 versions
Review web server access logs for unusual POST requests to Livewire component endpoints (e.g., /livewire/update) from unexpected IP addresses or with unusually large payloads
Implement Web Application Firewall (WAF) rules to detect and block PHP object injection patterns in Livewire snapshot parameters
Check for signs of compromise: new PHP files in web directories, unauthorized database access, unusual outbound network connections from web servers
simple_summary.why_this_is_serious: Laravel Livewire is embedded in millions of production web applications worldwide, and the vulnerability requires no authentication whatsoever — any internet user can attempt exploitation with a single HTTP request. The availability of public proof-of-concept tools and active exploitation by a nation-state APT (MuddyWater) that targets critical infrastructure makes this a high-priority threat. A successful attack grants full control of the web server, access to databases containing customer data, and a foothold for lateral movement into internal corporate networks. Many organizations remain unpatched nearly 9 months after the initial disclosure.

tactics.description: Attackers exploit CVE-2025-54068 in internet-facing Laravel Livewire web applications by sending crafted HTTP POST requests to the component update endpoint, requiring no authentication or valid session

tactics.technique_name: Command and Scripting Interpreter: Unix Shell
tactics.description: Post-exploitation commands are executed on the web server via PHP code injection triggered through the deserialized payload, allowing shell command execution with web server process privileges
MuddyWater actors observed deploying PHP webshells to compromised web servers following initial exploitation, providing persistent backdoor access independent of the original vulnerability
After achieving code execution as the web process user, attackers leverage local privilege escalation techniques to gain root/system access on the compromised web server
Web server compromise provides direct access to Laravel .env configuration files containing database credentials, API keys, session secrets, and other application credentials
Using credentials and network access obtained from the compromised web server, attackers move laterally to database servers, internal APIs, and other networked resources in the target environment

detection_guidance.summary: Detection focuses on anomalous POST requests to Livewire component endpoints, PHP deserialization patterns in web logs, and post-exploitation indicators such as new PHP files and unexpected outbound connections. Web Application Firewall (WAF) rules for PHP object injection are an effective preventive control. Organizations should also audit composer.json/composer.lock files to identify Livewire version across all PHP applications.

log_sources.description: Monitor POST requests to /livewire/update endpoint. Legitimate Livewire traffic includes session tokens and valid component snapshots. Malformed or oversized snapshot parameters are exploitation indicators.
WAF rules can detect PHP object serialization patterns (O:, a:, s: prefixes) in POST body parameters targeting Livewire endpoints. Alert on PHP class instantiation attempts via serialized payloads.
Monitor web document root directories for newly created PHP files. Webshell deployment following exploitation typically creates new .php files in publicly accessible directories.

log_sources.source: Outbound Network Connections from Web Servers
log_sources.description: Web servers making unexpected outbound connections (particularly on non-standard ports or to external IP addresses) indicate post-exploitation command and control activity.

what_to_look_for.indicator: POST request to /livewire/update with serialized PHP object in snapshot parameter
what_to_look_for.description: The core exploitation attempt sends a malicious serialized PHP object in the snapshot field of the Livewire component update endpoint. Look for PHP serialization prefixes (O:, C:) in request bodies.
what_to_look_for.log_field: http.method='POST' AND http.request_uri CONTAINS '/livewire/update' AND http.request_body CONTAINS 'O:'

what_to_look_for.indicator: Unexpected PHP file creation in web document root
what_to_look_for.description: Successful exploitation often results in webshell deployment. Monitor for new .php files created outside of normal application deployment processes.
what_to_look_for.log_field: file.path CONTAINS '.php' AND file.action='create' AND file.path CONTAINS '/var/www/'

what_to_look_for.indicator: Web server process spawning unexpected child processes
what_to_look_for.description: Code execution via PHP deserialization causes the web server process (www-data, apache) to spawn system commands such as bash, sh, curl, or wget.

what_to_look_for.indicator: Outbound connection from web server to unfamiliar IP addresses
what_to_look_for.description: Post-exploitation reverse shells and C2 beaconing involve outbound TCP connections from the web server process to attacker-controlled infrastructure.
what_to_look_for.log_field: network.direction='outbound' AND process.name IN ('php', 'php-fpm') AND destination.ip NOT IN trusted_networks

immediate_actions.action: Update Laravel Livewire to Version 3.6.4 or Later
immediate_actions.description: All PHP applications using Laravel Livewire v3 must be updated to version 3.6.4 or above. Run composer update livewire/livewire and test in staging before deploying to production. This is the only definitive fix — no workaround fully mitigates the risk.
immediate_actions.command: composer update livewire/livewire && composer show livewire/livewire | grep versions

immediate_actions.description: Identify all PHP applications in your environment using vulnerable Livewire versions. Check composer.lock files for livewire/livewire entries. Organizations often have multiple applications across development, staging, and production that all need patching.

immediate_actions.action: Deploy WAF Rules for Livewire Endpoint Protection
immediate_actions.description: While patching is underway, deploy Web Application Firewall rules to block PHP deserialization attacks against Livewire endpoints. Block POST requests to /livewire/update containing PHP serialization prefixes (O:, C:, a:). This provides temporary mitigation until all applications are patched.

immediate_actions.action: Check for Webshells and Signs of Compromise
immediate_actions.description: For applications that may have been running vulnerable Livewire versions for an extended period, perform a forensic review. Search web document roots for recently created PHP files, review access logs for exploitation patterns, and check for unauthorized database queries or user account creation.

immediate_actions.action: Rotate All Application Secrets and Database Credentials
immediate_actions.description: If there is any possibility your Laravel application was exposed to exploitation (i.e., was running a vulnerable Livewire version with internet exposure), immediately rotate all credentials in the .env file: database passwords, API keys, APP_KEY, session secrets, and any third-party service credentials stored in the application.
